smash.LOW ELBOW: Raise racket arm to shoulder level. Your shoulder angle is currently below 120°, which limits power and creates a 'T-Rex arm' swing.
smash.KINETIC CHAIN COLLAPSED: Keep non-racket arm up during the preparation phase to balance the body and facilitate torso rotation.
smash.FLAT-FOOTED: Initiate a scissor kick or active weight transfer. Your knees are too straight (over 165°), preventing explosive power from the legs.
smash.BENT ARM: Extend your arm fully at the point of contact. Ensure your elbow angle reaches at least 160° to maximize the height and steepness of the smash.

drop_shot.TELEGRAPHING: Increase your swing speed during preparation to match your smash velocity. Slowing down early allows opponents to predict the drop.
drop_shot.STIFF WRIST: Add a wrist snap at the point of contact. You need higher forearm rotational velocity to achieve a sharp downward angle over the net.
drop_shot.LEANING BACK: Shift your center of gravity forward. Your wrist is behind your shoulder, which pushes the bird upward rather than downward.
drop_shot.TOO STIFF: Soften your elbow slightly (below 175°) to provide better touch and control for the shuttle's placement.

serve.ELBOW TOO HIGH: Lower your racket arm shoulder angle (below 30°) to gain better control and a more consistent flight path.
serve.UNSTABLE STANCE: Bend your knees and widen your feet to lower your center of gravity and provide a stable base for the service motion.
serve.SERVICE FAULT RISK: Lower your contact point. The wrist height is currently above 120cm; contact must be made below the waist (waist-height) to be legal.
serve.EXCESSIVE MOVEMENT: Minimize torso rotation. Keep your body stable and quiet to ensure the shuttle clears the net by the smallest possible margin.
serve.COLLAPSED ELBOW: Keep your elbow extended (above 140°) to create a consistent pendulum motion for the serve.

forehand.CONTACT TOO LATE: Move your contact point forward. You are currently hitting the shuttle behind your shoulder line; aim to hit in front of your body.
forehand.USE YOUR CORE: Engage your hips and torso. Your current rotation is below 5°, meaning you are hitting only with your arm instead of your whole body.
forehand.EXTEND ARM: Reach through the contact point. Your elbow is too bent (under 150°), which reduces your hitting radius and power.

backhand.TURN YOUR BACK: Rotate your shoulders away from the net (at least 30°). Facing the net prevents you from generating the necessary torque for a backhand.
backhand.ELBOW TUCKED: Lead the movement with a high elbow. Do not keep the elbow tucked against your ribs; it needs space to snap the racket forward.
backhand.THUMB UP: Adjust your grip. Ensure your thumb is pressed firmly against the flat side of the handle to provide leverage for the backhand flick.
backhand.REACH ACROSS: Extend your arm across your body. You are currently hitting too close to your shoulder, which prevents a full swing arc.
